text: Caller claims to be from the IRS and says you owe back taxes that must be paid immediately, threatening that a warrant will be issued for your arrest or that law enforcement is on the way if you do not pay right now.

metadata.label: IRS back-taxes arrest threat
metadata.source: IRS - Beware of scammers posing as the IRS; FTC consumer alert

text: Caller poses as an IRS agent and instructs you to pay a tax bill or penalty by buying gift cards or prepaid debit cards from a store, then reading the card numbers and PINs over the phone. The real IRS never asks for or accepts gift cards as payment.

metadata.label: IRS gift-card payment demand
metadata.source: IRS - How taxpayers can protect themselves from gift card scams

text: Caller from an official-sounding but fake agency such as the 'Tax Resolution Oversight Department' or 'Tax Mediation and Resolution Agency' offers to fix a tax problem, do a 'red flag check' on your credit, or enroll you in an 'IRS liability reduction program', and leaves voicemails saying 'this may be our only attempt to reach you' to pressure a fast callback.

metadata.label: Fake tax-resolution agency
metadata.source: FTC - Hang up on unexpected calls saying you owe back taxes

text: Caller claims to be from the Social Security Administration and says your Social Security number has been suspended or linked to a crime, or that your benefits will end, unless you confirm your number or pay immediately. The real SSA never suspends numbers or demands payment.

metadata.label: Social Security number suspended
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Government Impersonation Scam

text: Caller impersonates a government agency such as the FTC, Medicare, or a court, and tells you their phone number on your caller ID proves they are legitimate. Caller ID is easily spoofed and is not proof of identity.

metadata.label: Government imposter (caller ID)
metadata.source: FTC - How to spot a government impersonator scam

text: Caller pretends to be a police officer, sheriff, or federal agent and says there is a warrant for your arrest, that you missed jury duty, or that you must pay a fine immediately to avoid being taken into custody.

metadata.label: Police arrest-warrant threat
metadata.source: FTC - Government impersonation scams; USAGov imposter scams

text: Caller insists you can only pay by going to a store like Target, Walmart, or CVS, buying gift cards, and reading the numbers on the back over the phone. No legitimate agency or business takes payment this way.

metadata.label: Gift-card payment demand
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam; IRS gift card scam guidance

text: Caller demands you wire money through Western Union or MoneyGram to resolve an urgent problem. Wire transfers are favored by scammers because they are fast and nearly impossible to reverse.

metadata.label: Wire-transfer payment demand
metadata.source: FTC - Scammers Use Fake Emergencies To Steal Your Money

text: Caller tells you to pay by sending cryptocurrency or depositing cash into a Bitcoin ATM. Government agencies and legitimate businesses never require payment in cryptocurrency.

metadata.label: Cryptocurrency payment demand
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam

text: Caller insists you pay immediately using a payment app like Zelle, Venmo, or Cash App to a number or handle they provide, framing it as the only acceptable method.

metadata.label: Payment-app (Zelle/Venmo) demand
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam

text: Caller tells you to withdraw cash or buy gold or precious metals and hand it to a courier who will come to your home, or mail it to an address, to 'keep it safe' or settle a debt.

metadata.label: Cash/gold courier pickup
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam (never withdraw cash or buy gold)

text: Caller pretends to be your grandchild or other relative in distress, saying they were in an accident or arrested and are in jail, and they urgently need money for bail or legal fees.

metadata.label: Grandparent bail emergency
metadata.source: FTC - Family Emergency Imposter Scams (video transcript)

text: Caller posing as a relative in trouble begs you not to tell other family members because they are embarrassed or scared, isolating you so no one can verify the story.

metadata.label: Secrecy ('don't tell anyone')
metadata.source: FTC - Family Emergency Imposter Scams; Scammers Use Fake Emergencies

text: Caller opens with a vague line like 'It's me, your grandson' or 'Grandma, do you know who this is?' without giving a name, waiting for you to supply a name they can then adopt.

metadata.label: Name-fishing ('guess who')
red_flags: do you know who this is
metadata.source: FTC and consumer guidance on name-fishing in grandparent scams

text: Caller claims to be a lawyer, public defender, doctor, or police officer calling on behalf of your relative who is supposedly in jail or the hospital, and relays urgent demands for money so you cannot speak to the relative directly.

metadata.label: Lawyer/officer relay for relative
red_flags: your grandson is in the hospital
metadata.source: ACE - Grandparent Scheme; FTC fake emergency guidance

text: Caller claims to be from your bank's fraud department and says they detected suspicious activity or an unauthorized transfer on your account that must be dealt with immediately, using manufactured urgency to stop you from verifying.

metadata.label: Bank fraud-department alert
metadata.source: FTC - Got a call about fraud activity on your bank account?

text: Caller tells you to move or transfer your money to a 'safe account' to protect it from fraud. Real banks never ask you to move money to protect it; moving it means losing it.

metadata.label: Move money to 'safe account'
metadata.source: FTC - Never move your money to protect it. That's a scam

text: Caller asks you to read back a one-time verification code that was just texted to you, claiming it is needed to confirm your identity or stop fraud. No legitimate bank or company ever asks for your verification code.

metadata.label: One-time verification code request
metadata.source: FTC - What's a verification code and why would someone ask me for it?

text: Caller pressures you to confirm or provide your full Social Security number, date of birth, or full account number, often claiming it is needed to verify your identity or fix an account problem.

metadata.label: SSN / personal-info request
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam; IRS imposter guidance

text: Caller asks for your online banking username, password, or security questions, or tells you to read them aloud so they can 'access your account and help you'.

metadata.label: Password / login request
metadata.source: FTC - Never move your money (never share username, password, or security codes)

text: A full-screen pop-up or call claims your computer is infected with a virus or sending your data to hackers, and instructs you to call a toll-free tech support number immediately or your computer will be disabled. Real error messages never include a phone number to call.

metadata.label: Tech-support virus pop-up
metadata.source: Microsoft - Protect yourself from tech support scams; FTC tech support alert

text: Caller claiming to be from Microsoft, Apple, or Geek Squad asks you to install remote-access software so they can connect to your computer to 'fix' a problem, which lets them install malware or steal information.

metadata.label: Remote-access request
red_flags: let me connect to your computer
metadata.source: Microsoft - Tech support scams; FTC guidance

text: Caller says you are owed a refund for an expired subscription or that they accidentally refunded you too much, then asks for remote access or bank login to 'reverse' the overpayment, tricking you into sending money back.

metadata.label: Refund overpayment scam
metadata.source: FTC and Microsoft tech support refund scam guidance

text: Caller says you have won a lottery, sweepstakes, or prize, but you must first pay taxes or a processing fee before you can collect the winnings. You never have to pay to receive a real prize.

metadata.label: Lottery/prize advance fee
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Government Impersonation Scam (lottery)

text: Caller pretends to be from your electric, gas, or water company and threatens to shut off your service within the hour unless you pay an overdue bill immediately, usually demanding a gift card or prepaid card.

metadata.label: Utility shut-off threat
metadata.source: FTC / USAGov - utility company impersonation scams

text: Caller or text claims to be from Amazon, USPS, UPS, or FedEx about a problem with a package or an order you didn't place, urging you to confirm details, pay a small fee, or call back to dispute a charge.

metadata.label: Package/delivery problem
metadata.source: FTC consumer alerts; USAGov imposter scams

text: Caller creates intense time pressure, insisting you must act right now, stay on the line, and not hang up, framing every second of delay as a threat so you cannot stop and think or verify.

metadata.label: Urgency / time pressure
metadata.source: FTC - How To Avoid a Scam (scammers create a sense of urgency)

text: Caller tells you to keep the call confidential and not discuss it with family, your bank teller, or anyone else, because telling someone would expose the scam.

metadata.label: Secrecy / isolation
metadata.source: FTC and consumer guidance on isolation tactics

text: Caller refuses to identify themselves, will not provide a callback number or case reference you can independently verify, or gets hostile and pressures you when you ask to call the company back on its official number.

metadata.label: Refusal to verify identity
metadata.source: FTC - verify by calling the number on your statement, not the caller's

text: Caller relies on a spoofed caller ID showing a real agency or bank name and number to convince you the call is genuine, then makes requests a real institution would never make.

metadata.label: Spoofed caller ID
red_flags: it says government on caller ID
metadata.source: FTC - Even if caller ID says it's the government, it could be a scam
